subject: ap-chemistry
unit_name: Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A bond in which electrons are shared unequally between two atoms because of a difference in electronegativity

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A structural diagram that shows all valence electrons in a molecule as dots and bonding pairs as lines between atoms

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The hypothetical charge on an atom in a molecule assuming bonding electrons are split equally between atoms

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The phenomenon where a molecule's electron structure cannot be accurately represented by a single Lewis structure and is better described as a blend of two or more structures

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The theory that predicts molecular geometry by minimizing repulsion between electron pairs around a central atom

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The mixing of atomic orbitals to form new orbitals of equal energy used for bonding; $sp$, $sp^2$, and $sp^3$ are examples

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A covalent bond formed by end-to-end orbital overlap along the internuclear axis; the first bond between two atoms is always this type

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A covalent bond formed by side-to-side (lateral) overlap of p orbitals, creating electron density above and below the bond axis

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The number of bonding pairs shared between two atoms; a single bond = 1, double = 2, triple = 3

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The average energy required to break one mole of a specific type of bond in the gas phase

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The average distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms at the point of minimum potential energy

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The bonding model describing metals as positive ions surrounded by a delocalized 'sea' of mobile electrons

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The bond type formed when one atom transfers electrons to another, producing oppositely charged ions that attract each other

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in a molecule as predicted by electron pair geometry and VSEPR theory

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A covalent bond in which electrons are shared equally because the two bonded atoms have similar electronegativities

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The type of hybridization present on a carbon atom forming four single bonds; involves one s and three p orbitals

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Proposed the electron dot structure method for representing valence electrons and chemical bonds in molecules

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Developed the electronegativity scale (0–4) widely used to predict bond polarity between atoms

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Developed the theory that electron pairs around a central atom repel each other to determine molecular geometry

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A molecule has the formula $AX_3E_1$ (3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair). What is the correct molecular geometry?

choices.explanation: Trigonal planar ($AX_3$) has 3 bonding pairs and NO lone pairs on the central atom. The lone pair in $AX_3E_1$ pushes the bonding pairs downward, creating a different shape.

$AX_3E_1$ has 4 electron groups (tetrahedral electron geometry) but one is a lone pair. The three bonding pairs form a pyramid shape with the lone pair occupying the apex position above the central atom — like ammonia ($NH_3$).
Tetrahedral ($AX_4$) has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs. When a lone pair replaces one bonding position, the molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal, not tetrahedral.
T-shaped geometry ($AX_3E_2$) requires 5 electron groups with 2 lone pairs. $AX_3E_1$ has only 4 electron groups total.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Why does a C=C double bond have a shorter bond length and greater bond energy than a C–C single bond?

choices.text: Double bonds involve more electron density between atoms, pulling the nuclei closer together and requiring more energy to break
choices.explanation: A double bond consists of one sigma bond and one pi bond. The additional electron density between the nuclei in the pi bond increases nuclear attraction, shortening the bond length and requiring more energy to completely break both bonds.

choices.text: Double bonds form between larger atoms that have stronger nuclear attractions
choices.explanation: Bond length and energy depend on the number of bonding pairs and nuclear attraction, not atom size. C=C and C–C both involve the same element — the difference is only in bond order.

choices.text: Double bonds prevent electron repulsion more effectively than single bonds
choices.explanation: Electron repulsion increases with more electron density between atoms. The shorter, stronger nature of double bonds is due to increased nuclear attraction from more bonding electrons, not reduced repulsion.

choices.text: Single bonds have sigma bonds while double bonds only have pi bonds
choices.explanation: Single bonds consist of one sigma bond. Double bonds consist of one sigma bond AND one pi bond — not just pi bonds. The sigma bond is always the first bond formed.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: What hybridization does the carbon atom have in carbon dioxide ($CO_2$)?

choices.explanation: sp³ hybridization involves 4 hybrid orbitals for 4 electron groups. Carbon in CO₂ has only 2 electron groups (two double bonds, no lone pairs), so only 2 hybrid orbitals are needed.
sp² hybridization involves 3 hybrid orbitals for 3 electron groups (as in ethylene C₂H₄). CO₂ carbon has only 2 electron groups, so sp hybridization applies.
Carbon in CO₂ forms two double bonds (O=C=O) with no lone pairs on carbon. Two electron groups require sp hybridization (2 hybrid orbitals at 180°), giving the linear geometry of CO₂.
d-orbital hybridization is not assessed in AP Chemistry and does not apply to carbon, which uses only s and p orbitals in hybridization.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following molecules is polar despite having two polar bonds?

choices.explanation: CO₂ has two C=O polar bonds that are equal in magnitude and point in exactly opposite directions (linear geometry). The bond dipoles cancel, making CO₂ nonpolar overall.
Water has two O–H polar bonds and a bent geometry due to two lone pairs on oxygen. The bond dipoles point at an angle and do not cancel, so H₂O has a net molecular dipole moment — it is polar.
BeCl₂ has two Be–Cl polar bonds in a linear geometry (sp hybridization). The dipoles cancel each other, making it nonpolar overall.
CCl₄ has four C–Cl polar bonds arranged in a tetrahedral geometry. The four bond dipoles are symmetrically arranged and cancel exactly, making the molecule nonpolar.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A central atom has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair. What is the correct molecular geometry?

choices.explanation: Tetrahedral molecular geometry occurs when there are 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs. When one of the four electron groups is a lone pair, the molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal.

Four electron groups (3 bonding + 1 lone pair) give tetrahedral electron geometry, but the molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal because molecular geometry only counts where the atoms are — the lone pair is invisible in the shape name.
Trigonal planar requires 3 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs. The presence of a lone pair changes the electron geometry to tetrahedral and the molecular geometry to trigonal pyramidal.
Bent geometry (AX₂E₂) has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs, like H₂O. Three bonding pairs and 1 lone pair gives trigonal pyramidal geometry.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Why does $N_2$ have a higher bond energy than $O_2$?

choices.text: Nitrogen has a higher atomic mass than oxygen
choices.explanation: Nitrogen (14 g/mol) actually has a lower atomic mass than oxygen (16 g/mol). Bond energy is determined by bond order and nuclear charge, not atomic mass.

choices.text: $N_2$ has a triple bond (bond order 3) while $O_2$ has a double bond (bond order 2)
choices.explanation: N₂ contains a triple bond (1 sigma + 2 pi bonds, bond order = 3) with bond energy ~945 kJ/mol. O₂ contains a double bond (bond order = 2) with bond energy ~498 kJ/mol. Higher bond order → more electron density between nuclei → stronger bond.

choices.text: Nitrogen atoms are closer together due to smaller atomic radius
choices.explanation: While bond length correlates with bond energy, the root cause is the bond order. It is the triple bond in N₂ that makes it both shorter and stronger than the double bond in O₂.

choices.text: $O_2$ has unpaired electrons that weaken the bond
choices.explanation: O₂ does have unpaired electrons (it is paramagnetic), but this does not weaken the bond in the simple VSEPR/Lewis model. The key factor is the lower bond order of O₂ (double) compared to N₂ (triple).

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: What is the formal charge on the nitrogen atom in $NH_4^+$?

choices.explanation: In NH₄⁺, nitrogen has 4 bonds and 0 lone pairs. Formal charge = valence electrons − lone pair electrons − (1/2)(bonding electrons) = 5 − 0 − (1/2)(8) = 5 − 4 = +1.
A formal charge of 0 would require nitrogen to be bonded as it is in neutral molecules. In NH₄⁺, nitrogen forms 4 bonds (not the usual 3), giving it a formal charge of +1.
A formal charge of −1 on nitrogen would occur if it had 6 non-bonding electrons or extra electrons in its account. In NH₄⁺, nitrogen has 0 lone pairs and 4 bonds, giving +1, not −1.
+4 is not a typical formal charge for nitrogen. Formal charge is calculated from valence electrons, lone pairs, and bonding electrons — it rarely exceeds ±1 or ±2.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which solid type has the highest melting point among ionic, molecular, metallic, and covalent network solids?

choices.explanation: Molecular solids are held together by weak intermolecular forces (IMFs) rather than chemical bonds. They have the lowest melting points among the four solid types.
Ionic solids have high melting points due to strong electrostatic attractions between ions, but they are generally lower than covalent network solids, which require breaking covalent bonds to melt.

Covalent network solids (like diamond and SiO₂) consist of atoms connected throughout by covalent bonds. Melting requires breaking covalent bonds across the entire structure, requiring extremely high temperatures — diamond melts above 3500°C.

Metallic solids vary in melting point (mercury is liquid at room temperature, tungsten melts at 3422°C), but covalent network solids as a category have the highest melting points due to pervasive covalent bonding.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes resonance structures of a molecule?

choices.text: Resonance structures represent different molecules that exist in equilibrium with each other
choices.explanation: Resonance structures are NOT separate molecules in equilibrium. They are different drawings of the same molecule where electrons are delocalized — the real structure is a blend of all resonance contributors.

choices.text: The actual structure of the molecule is an average (hybrid) of all resonance contributors
choices.explanation: Resonance structures are hypothetical Lewis structures. The true molecule is a resonance hybrid — intermediate between all contributors, with electron density spread (delocalized) across multiple bonds.

choices.text: The most stable resonance structure is the only structure that actually exists
choices.explanation: No single resonance structure 'actually exists.' All resonance structures contribute to the hybrid. The most stable contributor has the greatest influence, but the molecule is always the hybrid.

choices.text: Resonance structures must have different atom positions
choices.explanation: Resonance structures must have identical atom positions — only electron pair positions differ. Moving atoms would create a different molecule (isomer), not a resonance structure.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following molecules has $sp^2$ hybridization on its central atom?

choices.explanation: Carbon in methane forms 4 single bonds with no lone pairs, giving 4 electron groups. Four electron groups correspond to sp³ hybridization.
Boron in BF₃ forms 3 bonds with no lone pairs, giving 3 electron groups. Three electron groups require sp² hybridization, producing the trigonal planar geometry of BF₃.
Nitrogen in NH₃ has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair = 4 electron groups → sp³ hybridization, resulting in trigonal pyramidal geometry.
Oxygen in H₂O has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs = 4 electron groups → sp³ hybridization, resulting in bent molecular geometry.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: An element with electronegativity 3.5 bonds with an element with electronegativity 0.9. Which bond type best describes the bond between them?

choices.explanation: Nonpolar covalent bonds occur when the electronegativity difference is near 0 (typically < 0.4). A difference of 3.5 − 0.9 = 2.6 is far too large for nonpolar covalent.
Polar covalent typically applies to electronegativity differences of about 0.4–2.0. A difference of 2.6 is large enough to be classified as ionic.

An electronegativity difference of 2.6 (3.5 − 0.9) indicates a very large imbalance in electron sharing. This exceeds the threshold (~2.0) for ionic character, where electrons transfer rather than being shared.

Metallic bonding occurs in metal solids with delocalized electrons. A bond between two specific atoms with defined electronegativities is a chemical bond (ionic, polar covalent, or nonpolar covalent) — not metallic.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: In a Lewis diagram for $SO_3$, sulfur forms three double bonds (one with each oxygen). Which criterion best evaluates whether this or a resonance structure with single bonds is more accurate?

choices.text: The structure with the fewest bonds is always preferred
choices.explanation: Having fewer bonds does not indicate a preferred structure. Preferred resonance structures are chosen based on formal charge minimization, not bond count.

choices.text: The preferred structure minimizes formal charges, with negative formal charges on the most electronegative atom
choices.explanation: The best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges on all atoms and places any negative formal charge on the most electronegative atom. For SO₃, the structure with expanded octets on S (all double bonds) gives formal charges of 0 on all atoms, which is preferred over structures with formal charges of ±1.

choices.text: The structure that satisfies the octet rule for all atoms is always the preferred structure
choices.explanation: While the octet rule is a guideline, it can be violated for Period 3 and higher elements that can expand their octets. For SO₃, the expanded octet structure (zero formal charges) is preferred over the octet-satisfying structure with formal charges.

choices.text: Any Lewis structure for $SO_3$ is equivalent because resonance means all structures are equally valid
choices.explanation: Not all resonance structures contribute equally. Structures with lower formal charges and correct placement of charges are more significant (lower energy) contributors to the resonance hybrid.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: As bond order increases between two atoms of the same elements, which of the following correctly describes the change in bond length and bond energy?

choices.text: Bond length increases and bond energy increases
choices.explanation: Higher bond order increases bond energy (more bonds to break) but it shortens the bond length by pulling nuclei closer together, not lengthening it.

choices.text: Bond length decreases and bond energy increases
choices.explanation: Higher bond order means more electron density between nuclei, pulling them closer (shorter bond) and requiring more energy to break (higher bond energy). C≡C (bond order 3) is shorter and stronger than C=C (2) which is shorter and stronger than C–C (1).

choices.text: Bond length decreases and bond energy decreases
choices.explanation: While bond length correctly decreases with higher bond order, bond energy increases — a triple bond requires more energy to break than a single bond.

choices.text: Bond length increases and bond energy decreases
choices.explanation: This is the opposite of the actual trend. Higher bond order brings nuclei closer (shorter bonds) and requires more energy to break (higher bond energy).

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: An ionic compound dissolves in water. Which type of interaction describes how water molecules surround and stabilize the dissolved ions?

choices.explanation: London dispersion forces act between all molecules but are weak and temporary. They are not the primary interaction stabilizing ions in water, which involves directional electrostatic attractions.

When an ionic compound dissolves, the ions are surrounded by water molecules oriented so that the partial negative end of water (oxygen) faces cations and the partial positive end (hydrogen) faces anions. This ion-dipole interaction stabilizes the dissolved ions.
Covalent bonds involve electron sharing between atoms. Water does not form covalent bonds with dissolved ions; the interaction is electrostatic (ion-dipole), not electron-sharing.

Hydrogen bonding is an exceptionally strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on N, O, or F. While water molecules hydrogen bond with each other, the primary interaction between water and dissolved ions is ion-dipole.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following molecules has the greatest bond angle between the central atom and its bonding partners?

choices.explanation: H₂O has two lone pairs on oxygen that compress the H–O–H bond angle below the tetrahedral ideal (109.5°) to about 104.5°.
NH₃ has one lone pair on nitrogen that compresses the bond angles slightly below tetrahedral to ~107°, larger than H₂O but not the maximum.
Carbon in CO₂ has sp hybridization and linear geometry, giving O=C=O bond angles of exactly 180° — the largest possible bond angle.
Methane has tetrahedral geometry with 109.5° bond angles — larger than most bent or pyramidal molecules but not as large as the linear 180° angle of CO₂.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A student draws a potential energy vs. internuclear distance curve for two bonding atoms. At the minimum of the curve, which of the following is true?

choices.text: The atoms are at their maximum repulsion distance
choices.explanation: Maximum repulsion occurs at very short distances (left side of the curve), not at the minimum. The minimum represents the equilibrium bond length where attractive and repulsive forces are balanced.

choices.text: The atoms are at the equilibrium bond length with minimum potential energy
choices.explanation: The minimum of the potential energy curve corresponds to the equilibrium bond length — where the attractive forces between the nuclei and the shared electrons balance the repulsion between nuclei. This is the stable bond length.

choices.text: The potential energy is zero at the minimum
choices.explanation: Potential energy at the minimum is negative (below the zero reference level for infinitely separated atoms). The system releases energy when the bond forms, making the bonded state lower in energy.

choices.text: The atoms are moving apart because potential energy is decreasing
choices.explanation: At the minimum, the curve is flat (no net force), representing the equilibrium position. Atoms moving apart would be on the right side of the minimum where potential energy increases with distance.
